item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, ok) It's really hard to determine) I translated your article for Russian SCP Foundation and in Discuss there is discussion about who is who) In my interpretation this is a boy, so since this is a girl, I will correct my translation)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, didn't know that. In my mind she's a girl, but I guess it's up to reader interpretation since it's not clearly stated anywhere as far as I recall.

I actually named all of the characters in this after banana cultivars, so I didn't really think to look at how they correspond to actual people names.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Valery is a girl or boy? Cause as I know in France "Valery" is a masculine name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see where you were going with this, but this article feels a bit too much like SCP1322 for me. Unfortunate novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think "unpeel" is the closest word we have in the English language for…umm, "unpeeling".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Geez, Baseline Foundation. I get that Banana Foundation didn't want them coming back through, but they could've at least sent some bug spray, bug bombs, and systemic pesticides through for them to use >_>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agent Roger Smith
I think the easiest way to colonize an Alien planet would be genocide against the native population, preferably from orbit like they did in Ender's Game. I think its risky to engage in conventional warfare even if they have superior technology because loosing the war is always a possibility. Then the people who were being invaded have time to regroup, advance, and go to the enemy planet for revenge, just like what happened in Ender's Game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well the thing is Baseline wasn’t trying to colonize U-3567 the fruit flys where an accident but if they were to use SCP-2000 there would be a few problems

1. In order to cross dimensions you are required to peel the banana how would you get SCP-2000 a large machine along with the huge severs the Foundation stores the data of everyone on the planet to peel the banana as they can’t have all that being carried by one person.

2. SCP-2000 leaves humans with all the memories they have since their profile was last updated so how would the recently spawned humans react to waking up on a planet that is completely deferent then the one they were on “yesterday”

3. Using SCP-2000 would create a double of almost every human imagine the confusion that would create when dealing with the population assuming that you are importing goods from the U-3567 colonies

4. With all the humans suddenly spawning in on the alien world it is safe to assume that the panic would plunge the world into anarchy as they struggled to survive among the ruined wastes as the food from the banana corpses run out since without the bananas the local livestock would starve getting rid of the only possible food source and I believe that this chaos would make building a colony harder not easier

5. And finally the O5s would never approve moving something as vital to the Foundation as
SCP-2000 to another universe as it could be lost or damaged along the way there by dooming the baseline since that fact that they need SCP-2000 proves that without it they are doomed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I want to say that you have my respect Sebarus since in my reading I couldn’t tell that English was not your 1st language and I admire anyone that can speak multiple languages especially english since it is one if not the most complicated language still spoken and on top of that your article is definitely one of my favorite writing pieces since it got me thinking about cross-contamination of ecosystems and made me decide that if we encounter new lifeforms then it wouldn’t be like Star Trek where everyone goes down to the surface to meat the locals. Instead the way we would have to communicate is to be separated by layers of thick metal and glass or have inches of hazmat suit to protect the alien world from cross-contamination as even things like benign intestinal bacteria could be the deadliest biohazard when the whole evolutionary history of the planet didn’t have to deal with it and if your intent IS to colonize and you want to invade the planet remember that their diseases can work just as well as yours so you would have to fight in clunky hazmat suits while fighting an entire planet of beings while some of them may be sentient being with advanced civilization with an organized military that may have used unique resources on their planet to build weapons that you have no knowledge about so developing counter-measures would take years to make meanwhile your troops would be defenseless against it and on top of that you would have to deal with local wildlife that you have again no knowledge of until you have to deal with them while the locals would already have ways to deal with said wildlife (Something on our world would be bears we have items like bear mace and know to keep anything that has food in it or has had food in it up high out of the bears reach, while aliens would have to invent their own counter-measures after they get all their food eaten and have a lost a few people that got to close too a mother’s cub or made a sudden or aggressive movement)

Ok to wrap this up after getting off topic an invasion of another world that is already inhabited would be almost impossible and the only scenario I can think of that would end in a victory for the invaders is if the defending world has inferior military technology, lacks the ability to travel to the invader’s planet, the invaders can gather huge amounts of intelligence on everything the defenders do from building bombs to dealing with wildlife, and the invaders can transport soldiers and supplies in a efficient and cost-effective manner as they would need vast amounts of both, and to top all that they would need to create a airtight suit that can be effective in a combat situation (research into mechs would be recommended). I can make this list go on longer but I would need to write an essay to do the topic justice.


please be nice to our mobile reading friends

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is not about colonization, cause the intention of the baseline Foundation was not to colonize but too collaborate with the extra-dimensional Foundation but due too lack of foresight they accidentally brought something that was harmless and mundane from their dimension that caused a mass extinction in another. Since the baseline wasn’t using any force or had any plans to conquer this story is not a full repeat of the colonization of the New World that happened in our baseline. Instead it is a cautionary tale saying that when we discover new life, new worlds, or in this case a new dimension we must take the upmost care to prevent harmful cross-contamination as even day-to-day things that are only pests to us can be agents of mass destruction to a civilization that evolved separate from those things. The biggest tragedy of this all is that it could have been easily avoided if they used standard decontamination proceeders on either side of the dimensional portal or better yet completely seal of the portal chamber at first and have the base line bring their own oxygen and equipment to negotiate wirelessly and if in person contact or exchange of goods ever needed to happen then the use of the most rigorous decontamination protocol you have to ensure that a tragedy like this never happens.

(Note: I spent way too much time and effort on this comment probably because it’s my first thing I’m doing here as an actual member besides introducing myself but now I got a hopeful very insightful comment that is also very long and has took too much time that I’m making longer with this note so to wrap it up, internet please pass favorable judgment)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Following entry, SCP-4823 will unpeel itself.

Is this supposed to mean the peel closes over it again? Because the verbs "peel" and "unpeel" mean the same thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, this was REAL dark!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the discovery of the new world all over again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
one world's harmless normal creature is another's GK class end of Banana-ity scenario. I like that idea. I also like the diary entry. Valery had people she loved and plans for her future. She had a normal life she was living and watching that all get upended packed a real emotional punch.

I have some small gripes about the description. You wrote that the geography is different but Australia, Russia, France, and Portugal still exist. Also you could have further explored the nature of the foundation's diplomatic relations with U-3567. I want to know what scientific knowledge the foundation lost when they were cut off from that world and what kind of ally the other foundation would have been in containing SCPs. It would make the ending that much more tragic.

I think it wouldn't hurt to add an addendum about how the foundation is going to change its polices to prevent biological contamination of extradimensional places. People should be screened by medical staff for vermin and disease causing microbes before traveling. It would be like Nasa's planetary protection program

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All fair points. The reason for the banana people was not purely wackiness, though. I wanted the threat to be something completely unanomalous and ordinary, like fruit flies, which are possibly one of the most least threatening insects on the planet. Flies don't eat people though, so the people had to be fruits. I can, however, see why it might seem too nonsensical and take away from the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This piece feels like a diluted re-telling of SCP-1322 with unnecessary banana-flavoring. While I will admit I am disturbed by the notion of flies burrowing through ones flesh, the fact that it's, for what I can only assume is wackiness's sake, banana flesh only serves to distract from both the body horror and from the emotional impact of the pandemic. I also wasn't particularly a fan of the way the journal was written, which felt kitschy and somewhat plodding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, and congratulations to you because as far as I know you're the first person to realize that. I've been waiting for someone to discover that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's not a bad idea. Send SCP 2000 over there to repopulate there nearly dead planet with humans. They can complete the colonization. It would be like planet of the apes.
On a serious note, its possible U-3567's foundation has its own SCP 2000. fingers crossed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did some googling and realized that the people in Valery's journal are all named after different cultivars of bananas. That's a very nice touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That took a MASSIVE change in tone. The change from haha funny banana dimension travelling to a horror story was intense. Was not expecting that all but I loved it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I thought it was a reference to the Golden Temple from Donkey Kong Country Returns, but after reading, my only thought was "Why not send 2000 over there?"

The whole "foundation inadvertently destroys another reality" isn't anything new, but the way this is executed is still impressive, especially for a first article.

+1
this one was genuinely depressing and sad at times. I hope you will continue to write this is really good for a first article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of that one episode of Courage the Cowardly Dog with the banana people. There is similarly good imagery here, especially where the flies are concerned. However, the diary doesn't do it for me unfortunately. It's clear from the very start where the story is headed, and there isn't really enough character to offset this predictability. With the exception of the setting, it reads like a typical story of an apocalyse. I've seen it enough times that it no longer enthralls me like it used to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed. Thanks for pointing those out! And I really appreciate the kind words on my writing style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, my bad. I suppose that was a bit of an aggressive reading anyways.

Regarding tonal errors (which are fairly subjective and oftentimes only deal-breakers in aggregate):

+ show block
– hide block

Usage of "seem" and "appear" when those words imply that something is not actually true. Definitive language is preferable, because the banana actually does not suffer from normal biological degradation as opposed to merely seeming like it does not.

It is indestructible and does not seem to suffer from normal biological degradation. It appears to be ancient, with carbon dating placing it as at least 20,000 years old.

Small thing but consider capitalizing "addendum"?

See addendum 4823.1.

Usage of contractions in an official report.

On the second day, one of the representatives of the U-3567 Foundation didn't show up to a scheduled meeting.

Injecting irrelevant descriptions into a sentence; the damage sustained by the journal would be better served as its own sentence.

On 3/6/2020, SCP-4823 unpeeled itself and a journal damaged by moisture was ejected out of it.

Otherwise, other discrepancies from standard wiki writing conventions (e.g. "main anomaly" vs. "primary anomaly") can be marked up to writer's choice and your unique style. I actually do enjoy your writing style here, for what it's worth; it's in line with the conventions of clinical tone but still very distinctive.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't really have colonialism in mind when writing this, it stemmed more from the thought process of "what if animals never evolved and plants took their place, and what if a voracious, fast-breeding herbivore was then introduced to that ecology". There is some overlap between the concepts though, since conquistadors brought new diseases to the Americas that devastated much of the indigenous population.

I did think about ways of adding a little piece somewhere that would indicate some sense of guilt on the Foundation's part, but it felt detached.

When it comes to clinical tone errors, if you could point out some of those it would be greatly appreciated. English isn't my first language, so I sometimes struggle to differentiate clinical words from more casual ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Writing seems a bit off in places when the text is supposed to be clinical. Though, I do feel like a jerk for initially taking issue with the "bad" writing of the journal entries before realizing it's styled after indigenous sources of the same nature.

Ultimately, as a retelling of colonialism, the Foundation doesn't do much colonizing. The outbreak is just an unfortunate, holocaustic accident for which the colonizers are only at fault for their negligence. The descriptions of the horrors of the carvers are certainly riveting but I'd like to see more culpability on the part of the Foundation is all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I thought the hook was weak, but then I considered the effects of colonialism and how this skip embodies that. Good stuff, also the journal does a good job of bringing it home.

+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
First SCP! Thanks to Rounderhouse, RockTeethMothEyes, and Roget for crit. Also thanks to CadaverCommander and MaliceAforethought for helping with the details.

[Edit]
Here's the new image.
Original image: https://unsplash.com/photos/rIhCchNyves
Creator of Image:** Erik McClean on Unsplash
